section.title: David Anointed
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 15 ended with Samuel grieving over Saul's rejection as king. Chapter 16 opens with the LORD moving Samuel past that grief to Bethlehem, where He passes over Jesse's impressive older sons and chooses the youngest, a shepherd boy, because the LORD looks on the heart rather than the appearance. Samuel anoints David quietly among his brothers, and the Spirit of the LORD rushes upon him from that day forward, even as the Spirit departs from Saul, who is now troubled by a spirit of distress. Through David's skill with the harp, the newly anointed but still hidden king enters Saul's own household as a musician and armor bearer, quietly gaining a place at court long before Israel knows who he really is.
section.thesis: While Samuel and Israel are drawn to height and appearance, the LORD chooses and anoints the youngest, least likely son because He looks on the heart, and by His providence quietly places this hidden king in Saul's own household to be shaped for the throne he does not yet openly hold.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Samuel sent in secret to Bethlehem, verses 1 to 5; the brothers passed over because the LORD sees the heart, verses 6 to 10; the anointing of David and the Spirit rushing upon him, verses 11 to 13; and David's quiet entrance into Saul's service as the Spirit departs from Saul, verses 14 to 23.

verses.text: Now the LORD said to Samuel, “How long are you going to mourn for Saul, since I have rejected him as king over Israel? Fill your horn with oil and go. I am sending you to Jesse of Bethlehem, for I have selected from his sons a king for Myself.”
“How can I go?” Samuel asked. “Saul will hear of it and kill me!” The LORD answered, “Take a heifer with you and say, ‘I have come to sacrifice to the LORD.’
Then invite Jesse to the sacrifice, and I will show you what you are to do. You are to anoint for Me the one I indicate.”
So Samuel did what the LORD had said and went to Bethlehem. When the elders of the town met him, they trembled and asked, “Do you come in peace?”
“In peace,” he replied. “I have come to sacrifice to the LORD. Consecrate yourselves and come with me to the sacrifice.” Then he consecrated Jesse and his sons and invited them to the sacrifice.

units.label: Not as man sees
units.note: the LORD looks on the heart

verses.text: When they arrived, Samuel saw Eliab and said, “Surely here before the LORD is His {{mashiach|anointed}}.”

But the LORD said to Samuel, “Do not consider his appearance or height, for I have rejected him; the LORD does not see as man does. For man sees the outward appearance, but the LORD sees the {{lebab|heart}}.”
Then Jesse called Abinadab and presented him to Samuel, who said, “The LORD has not chosen this one either.”
Next Jesse presented Shammah, but Samuel said, “The LORD has not chosen this one either.”
Thus Jesse made seven of his sons pass before Samuel, but Samuel told him, “The LORD has not chosen any of these.”

And Samuel asked him, “Are these all the sons you have?” “There is still the youngest,” Jesse replied, “but he is tending the sheep.” “Send for him,” Samuel replied. “For we will not sit down to eat until he arrives.”
So Jesse sent for his youngest son and brought him in. He was ruddy, with beautiful eyes and a handsome appearance. And the LORD said, “Rise and anoint him, for he is the one.”
So Samuel took the horn of oil and anointed him in the presence of his brothers, and the {{ruach|Spirit}} of the LORD rushed upon David from that day forward. Then Samuel set out and went to Ramah.
Now the Spirit of the LORD departed from Saul, and a spirit of distress from the LORD began to torment him.
Saul’s servants said to him, “Surely a spirit of distress from God is tormenting you.
Let our lord command your servants here to seek out someone who can skillfully play the harp. Whenever the spirit of distress from God is upon you, he is to play it, and you will be well.”
And Saul commanded his servants, “Find me someone who plays well, and bring him to me.”
One of the servants answered, “I have seen a son of Jesse of Bethlehem who knows how to play the harp. He is a mighty man of valor, a warrior, eloquent and handsome, and the LORD is with him.”
So Saul sent messengers to Jesse and said, “Send me your son David, who is with the sheep.”
And Jesse took a donkey loaded with bread, a skin of wine, and one young goat and sent them to Saul with his son David.
When David came to Saul and entered his service, Saul loved him very much, and David became his armor-bearer.
Then Saul sent word to Jesse, saying, “Let David remain in my service, for I am pleased with him.”
And whenever the spirit from God came upon Saul, David would pick up his harp and play. Then Saul would find relief and feel better, and the spirit of distress would depart from him.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one, someone set apart to an office, especially king or priest, by the pouring of oil. Samuel first guesses Eliab is the LORD's mashiach by his looks alone; the true mashiach, David, is the youngest and least expected. The title points forward to ha-mashiach, the Anointed One, Jesus Christ, the true and final Anointed King and Priest.

lebab.gloss: Heart, the inner person, mind, will, and settled character, not merely emotion. Where man sees the outward appearance, the LORD sees the lebab, so David is chosen for who he is within rather than his stature or looks.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, wind, breath. The ruach of the LORD rushes upon David the moment he is anointed, empowering him for the calling God has appointed, in the same chapter that the ruach departs from Saul, marking a decisive transfer of God's enabling presence from the rejected king to the chosen one.

basic.context: Chapter 15 left Samuel grieving over Saul, whom the LORD had rejected as king. Chapter 16 opens with God moving Samuel past that grief into action, sending him to Bethlehem to anoint a successor from among Jesse's sons. Samuel expects the tall, impressive Eliab to be the one, but the LORD stops him: He does not choose the way people do. One by one, seven sons pass before Samuel, and none is chosen, until Jesse's youngest, forgotten out with the sheep, is sent for and anointed. From that day the Spirit of the LORD rushes upon David, even as it departs from Saul, who begins to be tormented by a spirit of distress. Through a court servant's recommendation, David, already anointed but not yet enthroned, quietly enters Saul's household as a musician whose playing soothes the troubled king. The key questions the chapter raises are why God passes over the obvious choices, what it means that the LORD looks at the heart, and why the anointed king remains hidden in humble service rather than immediately taking the throne.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing a real, one time anointing, not a formula guaranteeing that the youngest or least impressive person is always God's choice. What the chapter does teach as an enduring truth is how God evaluates people, by the heart rather than appearance or rank. The chapter also raises a fair question about the spirit of distress said to come from the LORD upon Saul, verse 14, which needs careful handling so that God's sovereignty over all things, even judgment, is affirmed without making Him the author of evil.
basic.originalLanguages: Samuel almost anoints Eliab as the mashiach, the anointed one, based on appearance, but the LORD corrects him: people judge by what they can see, while the LORD examines the lebab, the heart. When David is finally anointed, the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, rushes upon him from that day forward, the very moment the same Spirit departs from Saul, marking whose reign God is now building toward.
basic.typology: David is anointed king yet continues in quiet, hidden service, tending sheep and later serving in Saul's court, long before he visibly reigns. This pattern of an anointed one who is truly king yet lives for a time in humility and service before being openly enthroned points forward to Jesus, the true Anointed King who lived in humble obscurity and servanthood before His enthronement was made plain to the world.
basic.christ: David is chosen not for outward stature but because the LORD looks on the heart, and the Spirit rushes upon him at his anointing, equipping him for a reign that has not yet begun. In the same way, Jesus is God's true Anointed One, esteemed by the world as having no stately form or majesty, Isaiah 53:2, yet anointed with the Spirit without limit at His baptism, Luke 3:22, and truly King from the very moment of that anointing though His enthronement was not yet openly displayed. Where David waited years in hidden faithfulness before his coronation, Christ endured a season of humble obedience before being exalted to the throne at the Father's right hand.
basic.god: God is free and sovereign in whom He chooses, refusing to be bound by human standards of height, birth order, or appearance, and instead searching the heart. He is also sovereign over Saul's unraveling, a spirit of distress from the LORD troubling the king He has rejected, showing that even affliction and consequence remain under His hand rather than outside it. And He is a God who provides comfort in the midst of judgment, giving Saul relief through David's music even while working out His larger purpose of replacing him.
basic.crossReferences: First Samuel 15 explains why a new king is needed after Saul's disobedience. Psalm 78 later celebrates that God took David from tending the ewes to shepherd Jacob, His people. Paul's sermon in Acts 13 cites God's testimony that David was a man after His own heart. And Paul tells the Corinthians that God chose what is lowly and despised in the world, the same pattern seen in the choosing of the youngest son.
basic.oneStory: Israel asked for a king like the nations and received Saul, who is now rejected for his disobedience. Here the LORD begins to raise up a king after His own heart, anointing David while he is still young, unknown, and out with the sheep. The chapter is one more step in the long story of God choosing the humble and unlikely to carry His purposes, a pattern that reaches its fullness in Jesus, the true Anointed King born in humble circumstances and revealed in God's own time.

context.literary: The chapter is built around a deliberate reversal: Samuel, Jesse, and the reader are led to expect the tall, capable Eliab to be chosen, only for the LORD to overturn that expectation verse by verse as seven sons are rejected. The anointing itself is told briefly and quietly, in contrast to the extended build up, while the second half of the chapter shifts tone entirely, moving from the sacred hill country to the anguish of Saul's court, tying David's rise directly to Saul's decline.
context.historical: Bethlehem was a small town in Judah, and a family sacrifice there would draw little attention, allowing Samuel to anoint David without alerting Saul, who might have viewed it as treason. The horn of oil signified consecration to an office. Ancient Near Eastern courts commonly employed musicians for entertainment and, as here, for calming a troubled ruler, and a period of informal royal service, including as an armor bearer, was a normal way for a capable young man to gain standing.
context.canonical: This chapter begins the long story of David, the king after God's own heart whose line will culminate in the Messiah. It sits between Saul's rejection, chapter 15, and David's public rise, chapter 17, and its themes, God's choice of the humble, the anointing by the Spirit, and the contrast between outward appearance and the heart, recur throughout the historical books and are picked up directly by the New Testament's portrait of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD, not human judgment, determines who will reign, choosing David because of his heart rather than his appearance, and to trace how the newly anointed king is providentially placed within Saul's own court well before his throne is visible to Israel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical anointing and a specific instance of an afflicting spirit; it does not prescribe a method for identifying leaders today, nor does it teach that God routinely sends tormenting spirits on the disobedient. What endures is the principle that God evaluates the heart rather than appearance, and that He remains sovereign even over the difficult experiences of those who reject Him.

debates.question: What is the 'spirit of distress from the LORD' that torments Saul, verse 14?

views.label: A judicial affliction God directly sends
views.case: The text plainly says the spirit is from the LORD, and it parallels other passages, such as 1 Kings 22:19-23, where God sovereignly permits a spirit to bring about judgment on a rejected king; this reads Saul's torment as a direct consequence of his own disobedience, sent under God's authority as discipline.

views.label: God's sovereign withdrawal allowing an evil spirit's activity
views.case: Once the Spirit of the LORD departs, verse 14, protection and blessing are withdrawn, and a harmful spirit, as the footnote allows, is free to trouble Saul; God is sovereign over this happening, permitting rather than authoring the evil itself.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms that Saul's unraveling flows from his own rejection of God's word and that nothing, not even a tormenting spirit, operates outside God's sovereign authority; neither view makes God the author of evil, and both magnify His justice and His sovereign rule.

study.originalLanguages: Samuel looks at Eliab and is ready to call him the LORD's mashiach, the anointed one, simply because he looks the part, tall and impressive like Saul once was. The LORD stops him short: man looks at the outward appearance, but the LORD examines the lebab, the heart, the seat of character and will hidden from human eyes. Seven sons pass this test and fail it, until the youngest, forgotten with the sheep, is anointed, and the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, rushes upon him from that day forward. In the very next verses that same ruach departs from Saul, and a spirit of distress from the LORD begins to trouble him, so that the chapter's vocabulary itself traces the quiet transfer of God's empowering presence from the rejected king to the chosen shepherd.

typology.type: David anointed yet remaining in hidden, humble service
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the eternally appointed King, who lived in obscurity and servanthood before His kingship was openly displayed, Philippians 2:6-11.

typology.type: The Spirit rushing upon the anointed one
typology.fulfillment: The Spirit descending on Jesus at His baptism, anointing Him for His messianic ministry, Luke 3:21-22 and Luke 4:18.

typology.type: The LORD looking on the heart rather than outward appearance
typology.fulfillment: God's consistent pattern of choosing and accepting people by grace and inward reality, fulfilled in Christ, who sees and searches every heart, Revelation 2:23.

study.christ: David is chosen not for stature but because the LORD looks on the heart, and at his anointing the Spirit rushes upon him for a throne he will not visibly occupy for years. Jesus is the true ha-mashiach, the Anointed One, of whom Isaiah says He had no stately form that we should look at Him, Isaiah 53:2, yet He was anointed with the Spirit beyond measure at His baptism, Luke 3:22, John 3:34. Like David, Christ lived a season of hidden, humble obedience, working as a carpenter and serving others, before His kingship was openly revealed at His resurrection and ascension. Where David waited on God's timing rather than seizing Saul's throne, Christ likewise did not grasp at glory but humbled Himself, trusting the Father to exalt Him in His own time, Philippians 2:8-9.
study.god: God is sovereign and free in His choices, refusing to be bound by human measures of height, birth order, or appearance, and instead searching the heart of each person before Him. He remains sovereign even in Saul's decline, sending or permitting a spirit of distress upon the king He has rejected, so that even this difficult providence serves His purposes rather than standing outside His rule. And He is compassionate even in judgment, providing relief for Saul through David's music, weaving mercy into the very process by which He is replacing one king with another.

crossReferences.note: Saul's rejection for disobedience, the reason a new anointing is now needed.
The LORD took David from tending the ewes to shepherd Jacob, His people, with integrity of heart and skillful hands.
God chooses what is lowly and despised in the world, the same pattern seen in the choosing of the youngest son over his brothers.
Paul recounts God's testimony that He found in David a man after His own heart, who will do all His will.
A parallel account of a spirit sent by the LORD's permission in judgment, helping frame the spirit of distress that troubles Saul.

study.oneStory: Israel had asked for a king like the nations and received Saul, now rejected for his disobedience. Here the LORD begins openly to raise up a king after His own heart, anointing David while he is still young, overlooked, and tending sheep, and placing him quietly within Saul's own court long before Israel knows who he is. This is one more step in the long story of a God who chooses the humble and unlikely to carry forward His purposes, a pattern that reaches its fullness in Jesus, the true Anointed King, born in humble circumstances and revealed as King in God's own perfect time.

quiz.q: Why does the LORD reject Eliab even though Samuel is sure he is the one?

options.text: The LORD does not see as man sees; man looks at the outward appearance, but the LORD looks on the heart, v7.
options.note: This is the LORD's own explicit reason to Samuel in verse 7.

options.text: Samuel judged by outward stature, just as Israel once judged Saul, but God's standard for this king is different.
options.note: True and related, but it is a broader observation rather than the text's stated reason.

options.text: Eliab was too old and had already missed his opportunity to be chosen.
options.note: Age or birth order is not given as the reason; the stated reason is the heart, not appearance.

options.text: There was no reason given; the choice was arbitrary.
options.note: The LORD gives a clear reason, seeing the heart rather than appearance, so it is not arbitrary.

quiz.q: What happens the moment David is anointed, and what happens to Saul in the same chapter?

options.text: The Spirit of the LORD rushes upon David from that day forward, even as the Spirit departs from Saul, marking a transfer of God's empowering presence.
options.note: Verses 13-14 place these two events side by side deliberately.

options.text: David becomes instantly popular with all the people of Israel.
options.note: David's reputation does grow later in the chapter, but the immediate, stated result of the anointing is the Spirit rushing upon him.

options.text: David receives the Spirit only because he could already play the harp well.
options.note: His musical skill is mentioned afterward, but it is not the reason the Spirit comes upon him at his anointing.

options.text: Nothing changes for David; he simply returns to tending the sheep with no further mention of the Spirit.
options.note: The text explicitly says the Spirit rushed upon him from that day forward.

quiz.q: What does the spirit of distress that torments Saul reveal, verses 14-16?

options.text: It shows that even Saul's affliction after his rejection remains under the LORD's sovereign hand, not outside it.
options.note: The text names it a spirit of distress from the LORD, keeping God's sovereignty central even here.

options.text: It shows an evil power operating completely apart from any relationship to God's rule.
options.note: There is real trouble here, but the text ties its origin to the LORD's sovereign action or permission, not to a power independent of Him.

options.text: It shows that Saul was simply experiencing ordinary sadness unrelated to his rejection as king.
options.note: The narrative directly connects the affliction to the Spirit's departure after his rejection, not mere ordinary sadness.

options.text: It shows that David's music had no real effect on Saul at all.
options.note: Verse 23 says Saul found relief and felt better when David played.

quiz.q: Why was David initially left out when Jesse presented his sons to Samuel?

options.text: David, the youngest, was overlooked and left tending sheep until Samuel insisted every son be brought before him.
options.note: Verse 11 shows Jesse did not even think to include his youngest son at first.

options.text: Jesse deliberately hid David to protect him from being noticed by Saul.
options.note: The text gives no such motive; David was simply out with the sheep as the youngest son.

options.text: David refused to come because he did not want to be anointed.
options.note: David is sent for and comes; there is no refusal on his part in the text.

options.text: David was actually the firstborn and always expected to be chosen.
options.note: David is explicitly the youngest of the sons, the least expected choice.

quiz.q: What is significant about David entering Saul's service as a musician and armor bearer?

options.text: God's providence quietly placed the newly anointed but still hidden king in Saul's own household, gaining him experience and access to the court before Israel knew who he was.
options.note: The chapter shows David's anointing followed immediately by an unlikely, provident door into Saul's court.

options.text: David entered Saul's service purely for his musical talent, with no larger purpose in the narrative.
options.note: His music is the stated occasion, but the narrative places this within the larger story of God quietly establishing His anointed one.

options.text: David forced his way into Saul's service in order to seize power early.
options.note: David is sent for by Saul through a servant's recommendation; he does not force his way in.

options.text: David never actually entered Saul's service in this chapter.
options.note: Verses 21-23 explicitly describe David entering Saul's service and becoming his armor-bearer.

questions: Samuel almost anointed Eliab because of his impressive appearance. Where are you tempted to judge people, or even yourself, by outward appearance rather than the heart?
David was out tending sheep, overlooked even by his own family, at the very moment God had already chosen him. Where do you feel overlooked right now, and how might God be quietly at work in you?
The Spirit of the LORD rushed upon David the moment he was anointed, equipping him for a calling he would not step into for years. How is God preparing you today for something you cannot yet see?

Saul's spirit of distress is a sober reminder that turning from God can leave a person tormented even while still sitting on a throne. Is there an area where you have drifted from God and feel the resulting unrest?
David served faithfully in obscurity, as a musician and an armor bearer, long before he was crowned. What faithful, unseen service is God asking of you while you wait on what He has promised?

translationNotes.note: The LORD's words play on two Hebrew ideas: man judges by mar'eh, outward appearance, while the LORD examines the lebab, the heart, the inner seat of character and intention hidden from human sight.
The BSB footnote notes Shammah is a variant of Shimeah, Shimea, and Shimei found elsewhere for this brother, 2 Samuel 13:3, 2 Samuel 21:21, and 1 Chronicles 2:13.

The BSB footnote allows 'a spirit of distress' to be rendered 'a harmful spirit,' the same phrase recurring in verses 15, 16, and 23; the text affirms it comes from the LORD while showing it torments Saul rather than blessing him.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 16:1-23. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced elsewhere in this file.
